sentences.text: You see, sitting here in an apartment, in a comfortable apartment, 50, 55 years later, but when I'm telling you-- when I'm coming back to this time to where  I was looking on the circumstances there, and I got to convert myself.
I got to ignore the place where I am.
With my whole image, with my body, and with my mind, I am there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And then when I'm telling you, it's like it's real.
I'm 16 years old, naked in the haystack.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wasn't exposed to nature.
I was a city boy, when suddenly in one week, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] all I that I went through, losing everybody, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] getting [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] humiliated.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Handled-- even-- even-- even an animal, I think, people wouldn't handle an animal like they handle me.
In the beginning, the Judenrat, from my own people, and then from the Germans, why?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I try-- I try-- it came into my mind at that time, maybe I deserve that.
Why are all of the people, whoever they are, are they wrong and I'm right?
Or am I right, and they are wrong?
And it came to mind, why I am fighting [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to be alive?
Why-- I should be better deserving the [? whole ?] pain [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that I have sinned.
My sin is bigger than my brothers, or my father, or my sister?
Why am I not with them?
Why run away?
What do I need it all?
[SIGHS] Even the nature is against me-- birds.
They-- the German didn't catch me.
The birds got me.
So maybe somewhere I'm wrong.
I don't have to live.
I got to give up.
But when I'm alone, alone, I have nobody to ask, to talk, to consult.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Where am  I now?
What am I doing?
What's my next step?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
When the birds-- I-- I wasn't successful to chase them away.
I chased from my hair their on the bottom.
I am chasing them from the bottom, they're-- on my-- my upper-- upper body.
Then I slid back.
Instinctively, I slid back into the stack of the hay.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And I am laying there.
In the hay, they couldn't get me.
But I heard them around me-- caw, caw, caw.
They're talking to themselves, they'll get me.
When I have nothing to protect myself except inside this haystack.
I think, how long can I  stay here?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Probably, I fell asleep again tired-- to be tired.
I lost my consciousness-- pain all over my body.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I saw through the hay that it's dark, it started to be peaceful and quiet around me.
The birds disappeared.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came out.
Nobody behind me.
But I don't know where  I am.
I lost my sense of directions.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't know where-- what to start and where to start to go.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] That's that.
My conscious came back, a little to me.
And I started to go in the pits, out where I heard the sounds of dogs barking-- came to my ears sounds of human, of car, moving, [INAUDIBLE], horses.
I started to go to this direction.
Eventually, after half a mile, I came out to a dry field, coming out when I saw in front of me this house that I show you.
And then, I sleep over [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in this place.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In the early morning, I hear people coming closer-- the voice of people coming closer, and closer, and closer towards me.
And that's I find out looking out from the wall that was from the building itself, that was a distance.
And I could look out.
The sun [? kind of ?] shining.
I look out.
There's two peasants, two farmers, coming with a wagon and a horse, coming towards me.
And when they coming closing to me, they turning away.
When I watched him, I observe what they were doing.
I heard them talking.
But I find out they came in to catch the fish that was at the man-made [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] water where they used to feed fish.
And they would then catch them up, put them in the wagon.
This was a barrel on the wagon where they put the-- catch  of the fish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After they finished their work, they went away.
I came-- I go out to the place where the fish are, and I find a sack, an empty sack.
I put myself-- the sack on myself.
I make a hole.
And it was like a-- a dress on me, around my neck, not to be naked.
But that was so rough, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the sack on me, it was bothering me.
My body was full with wounds.
It would make me uncomfortable.
I took them off again.
I figure I'll wait till the evening.
I was afraid to go out and find out that where I am.
My conscious came back a little to myself.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And eventually, they went-- passed by the day.
And I start to feel like I'm hungry.
I would eat something.
It was already about two day, and I didn't have anything.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But I think it was two days-- it's wasn't two days.
It was probably was four or five days.
I cannot figure that out.
I don't know, because I got-- stomach was so empty, and I felt like I need something in my mouth.
I started to drink the water from the fish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came after myself a little-- come back to myself.
You know, when you are hungry, you don't think of dangers.
You do everything to grab something.
That was starting at the evening I came out right up to the land when I saw a farmer's house, a single farmer's house.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I saw the woman came out.
And she's milking the cows.
And she put it in a big-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in a big-- how to say-- how you call that in English.
In a canister.
That's right.
She put it in a big aluminum canister, the milk.
When she put it into the the well of cold water there, using the well for the water.
And they are putting it in to keep that cold.
I waited till they went in.
I pull out the milk.
I pull out the milk.
It was catched by a wire-- by a thread wire.
I pull out.
I drink the milk.
It was good milk.
It keeps-- makes me better feeling.
But I was a afraid to knock on the door or to not to knock on the door.
Eventually, I figure I don't know where to go.
Where ?] I was going, my plan was to go back to Juzia, to the same Polish girl.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I am afraid to ask, but I start to figure out when my conscience came back where I run-- run always here.
I ran away from here.
Juzia is here.
She is between [INAUDIBLE].
And when I figure it out, when I start to go the other direction, I figured I'd find her somehow.
And I'm going, and I'm going, and I'm going.
So all night, my luck was, it was always a little raining-- cold rain on my body.
I took off the sack because it was bothering me.
It was rubbing me on my body.
It  was hurting me.
Instead to protect me, it was making me more-- the wounds open.
And the boots also, they opened up the wounds that I got from the Germans.
They opened it up.
And it was leaking blood.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So it was bothering me.
So I complete naked.
Eventually, my sense of directions didn't help me to go to Juzia-- the same place where I ran away in the first place.
That was early in morning.
I was going all night in circles.
When I find out I am in the wrong direction, I go back.
But eventually, I find it.
With the sense of the directions, believe it or not, the dogs were barking by a farmer, they were giving me the signs where I am and what direction should I go.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The structure of all the farms give me an idea where and which directions I should go.
Eventually, I came to Juzia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I go in into the barn where she was keeping me before, after I went to Radun looking for my sister.
I know I had to open the secret opening from the gate.
When I got in, I laid down in the hay.
She didn't know that I am there.
But in the morning, I was watching for her and looking for her, and she was going out or her brother going to do something for the cows.
I knock.
She was out on the-- on the front to get some water from the well.
I opened the door.
And I yelled, Juzia, Juzia.
Eventually, she heard me.
She saw the door.
The gate opens up from the barn.
Who is in the barn?
So she come into the barn hesitating.
Who is that?
I say, Juzia, come here.
I ?] [? say, ?] [? yes. ?] But she saw me.
She says says, oh yes, [NON-ENGLISH].
That's you.
What happened to you?
Where were you?
I tell her the whole story where I went to Radun from [INAUDIBLE] till later, went to [INAUDIBLE].
The Germans catch me.
And here I am after whatever they did to me.
I am-- Juzia, I am [INAUDIBLE].
Whatever you want to do to me, do with me.
So she says, what do you mean whatever I want?
You're here.
You are in a safe place.
I'll take care of you.
She put compress, cold compress, on my whole body.
She healed me.
She was an angel for me.
She gave me food.
She feed me.
Of course she was in danger.
But she didn't think of any danger.
When she came, she-- she went to her brother.
They were two angels and such a nice guy.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They put-- they put my clothes on me.
They gave me accommodating, soft underwear, not-- not to hurt my body.
And she say, whatever will be with us is going to be with you.
Don't worry.
We'll take care of you.
Just be quiet.
Lay down.
And I was in this barn.
They make for me a shelter.
And they need the place where the horse was staying.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the horse has a room like a half the size of this room in the barn.

And he has a place-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know how to explain that-- for feeding the horse with hay, with [INAUDIBLE], whatever, with grains And to this place, they put-- they dig a grave, a hole, for me to stand up.
They put back all the-- the-- the-- everything underneath the place.
And I was laying there.
In case of danger, I should go in and lay there.
I was there till May, end of May, 1942.
This was September '41, eight months I was hiding in this place.
She was taking care of me.
She was-- I mean, a mother would-- wouldn't do-- couldn't do better cherishing me, giving me hope [INAUDIBLE] that I'm going to survive.
I said Juzia, you know the same words.
She knew my father from before.
She used the same words my father promised me that I will  stay alive.
And I'll survive.
Say what you are saying to me.
I said, I know you're going to survive.
And they both of us-- both of them, they were so good to me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In the meantime, eight months is a long time to be in hiding between the mouse and in darkness for the horse.
The horse was only  my companion.
I got used to him.
So he got used to me.
And that was a female horse.
She got pregnant.
When I came in, she was already pregnant three months later.
She brought-- ?] and we got-- that was my only friends, the little horse.
He got born.
He saw me finally between her and her mother, I'm a third person.
[LAUGHS] I got user to this horse.
We were pals-- friends.
She was licking me.
I was licking her.
The horse was licking me.
We were a  family, a family of horses.
I became a horse.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [LAUGHS] It's funny to say.
She was sucking her mother.
I tried to suck too.
She let me.
[LAUGHS] I-- I-- I was one of the family there with the horses.
[SIGHS] I-- I couldn't-- I couldn't remain in the closed place, even I got good food, shelter, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and security.
Good people were around me.
She used to come in a couple times a day to bring me food.
He used to come in special to keep me company, not to be  bored he used to tell me what's going on with the war.
He used to bring me newspapers.
But the papers were German papers printed in the Polish language.
And I saw, my god, instead of waiting for liberation that the war-- the war will end soon on my side, that they're going to lose.
I saw they going still forwards.
The Germans were occupying more eastward.
And eastward, they are taking Sevastopol.
They are taking Mariampol.
And they are taking [INAUDIBLE].
And they're taking-- Leningrad is surrounded already for two years.
Millions of people are dying there.
They are near Stalingrad.
They are near Moscow-- Kiev, Krakow-- all the cities are taken from the Germans.
They are coming every time.
And they like to keep my morale high.
But it-- it's-- it's not so according to reality.
So I figured all along can they keep me if the situation is against me?
And here in May '42, the same fate of Eishyshok came to Radun people-- Jews, Yanova Jews, and all the surrounding villages from white Russia, they got killed and massacred like [INAUDIBLE].
So there is no hope.
So I don't want to be too much of a burden for them.
All the people can only do for me is that much.
They are only people.
On the front, [INAUDIBLE] the Russian will-- should come back to-- to-- to the-- the-- the Germans are going forwards.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I figure after the massacre in Radun [INAUDIBLE], many Jews ran away into the forest.
So I figured when she's coming with  news, you know, the parent-- the-- the peasants, the farmers, they know everything.
These kind of Jews in this-- [? enabled ?] in these farms, in these pits.
They are in hiding.
And they organized themselves in a partisans group.
And they are  coming and they're taking foods, foods for them to feed himself.
And they are--
So I went this way.
So I figure let's look for them.
And I saw one time I said to Juzia, you know what?
It is enough.
You did for me that much.
I feel that's the maximum a human can do for another human and why [INAUDIBLE] eventually.
I'm just a little Jew.
I know you want me to help me.
But I figure I'll go to the rest of the Jews who are in hiding.
But in case, I wouldn't be able to manage there, I will come back to you.
So they say, both of them-- [?
Yusiska, ?] do whatever you wish.
Our door is open for you.
Whenever you come back, you know how to open the door in the barn.
Go in.
Be our guest.
We'll support you.
And we'll hide you.
But if you feel like to go back to the rest of the Jews that are hiding in the forest and the pits, do your wish.
And I left.
And I came back to the Jews [? faced-- ?] who ran away from the Radun Ghetto.
When I met survivors, one of them who hit me in my back-- in my face, he iddn't want to see me here when I ran away from Eishyshok.
So I telled-- I told him, [?
Froike-- ?] [?
Froim ?] was his name-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did you-- couldn't you foresee that the fate of Radun is going to be the same as Eishyshok?
You made me run away to Yanova.
And I went through the whole thing now I [INAUDIBLE].
Eight months I could live like a human like you did.
So I-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I didn't-- I mean, I didn't do him anything.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Before even the massacre in Radun, I hear that some Russian partisans organizing themselves about 15 kilometers from Juzia, where I was sitting.
Radun still remained a Jewish ghetto.
When I get out-- got out from the barn, I told him, I'm going to look for the partisans from the Russian, in [INAUDIBLE] for a week.
And like I tell you before, I got such a smell of directions.
I never been-- I-- I-- a farmer's boy.
I was a city boy.
But the smell of directions I have exactly where I am going and how to find my way.
And eventually, after a week of searching, I find Russian partisans.
In the beginning, they have organized themselves.
And who are the Russians?
The Russians wasn't partisans who sent by the Russian paratroops or what.
They were a main leftover.
They didn't go in-- into the-- into the surrounding-- they didn't surround to the Germans.
They were in hiding all the time.
In the daytime, the peasants, the farmers, they occupied them in helping them in the farming.
And at night, they used to hide in the forest.
So they didn't care too much.
It was easier for them to survive in the hiding than with the Jew together, because a Jew, a Pole, wouldn't let in a Jew in hiding.
A Russian is that kind of people.
They used to use them in daytime-- give them food.
And then at nighttime, they were in hiding in the forest.
But they have more or less an idea where to hide when the future organized themselves into groups and to bigger groups.
And little by little, they organized themselves.
We're going to pause here to change the tape.
